article.title: Microsoft Open-Sources 86-DOS 1.00, the Earliest DOS Source Code Ever Found, Transcribed From Tim Paterson's Garage Printouts
article.summary: On 86-DOS 1.00's 45th anniversary, Microsoft released the earliest DOS source code discovered to date under the MIT license, including kernel sources and PC-DOS 1.00 development snapshots.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Microsoft has released the source code to 86-DOS 1.00 — the direct ancestor of MS-DOS — under an open source license, marking the 45th anniversary of the operating system that became the foundation of the IBM PC software ecosystem. The release was announced on April 28, 2026 by Microsoft's Stacey Haffner and Scott Hanselman in a [Microsoft Open Source Blog](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/28/continuing-the-story-of-early-dos-development/) post titled "Continuing the story of early DOS development."

Writing on the company's open source blog, Haffner and Hanselman framed the release as part of an ongoing preservation effort: "Today, on 86-DOS 1.00's 45th anniversary, we're continuing that tradition by preserving the earliest DOS source code discovered to date," they wrote on the [Microsoft Open Source Blog](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/28/continuing-the-story-of-early-dos-development/).

## What Was Released

The code drop, hosted in the `DOS-History/Paterson-Listings` repository on GitHub, covers more than just one kernel. According to the [Microsoft Open Source Blog](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/28/continuing-the-story-of-early-dos-development/), "The listings include sources to the 86-DOS 1.00 kernel, several development snapshots of the PC-DOS 1.00 kernel, and some well-known utilities such as CHKDSK." [It's FOSS](https://itsfoss.com/news/ms-dos-early-code-open-sourced/) reports that the assembler used to write the operating system was also published as part of the same release.

The code is licensed under the MIT license, according to both [It's FOSS](https://itsfoss.com/news/ms-dos-early-code-open-sourced/) and [GamingOnLinux](https://www.gamingonlinux.com/2026/04/more-retro-goodies-microsoft-open-sources-86-dos-and-pc-dos/), which also confirms the GitHub location at `DOS-History/Paterson-Listings`.

## How the Source Survived

Unusually for a major open-source release, the published code did not come from a corporate version-control system — it was reconstructed from paper. "The earliest DOS source code was found on printer paper in Tim Paterson's garage," [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/microsoft_pc_dos_1_point_oh/) reports, referring to the original author of 86-DOS. [GamingOnLinux](https://www.gamingonlinux.com/2026/04/more-retro-goodies-microsoft-open-sources-86-dos-and-pc-dos/) quotes Microsoft's announcement directly: "The earliest DOS source code was found on printer paper in Tim Paterson's garage so we've open sourced it on 86-DOS 1.00's 45th anniversary!"

Paterson had retained physical assembler printouts and continuous-feed paper from 1981, according to [It's FOSS](https://itsfoss.com/news/ms-dos-early-code-open-sourced/), which notes that historians "had to locate, scan, and transcribe the DOS-related portions into compilable code." The work was credited to preservationists Yufeng Gao and Rich Cini, who are named in the [Microsoft Open Source Blog](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/28/continuing-the-story-of-early-dos-development/) post and in coverage by [OSnews](https://www.osnews.com/story/144849/earliest-86-dos-and-pc-dos-code-released-as-open-source/).

The Microsoft authors used the release to argue for a broader view of what software preservation looks like. "Software history lives in code, yes, but also in scanned listings, internal documents, assembler printouts," Haffner and Hanselman wrote on the [Microsoft Open Source Blog](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/28/continuing-the-story-of-early-dos-development/).

## Historical Context

86-DOS was originally written by Tim Paterson and was released in 1980, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/microsoft_pc_dos_1_point_oh/). Microsoft licensed the operating system and then purchased all rights in 1981, before the IBM PC's release. [It's FOSS](https://itsfoss.com/news/ms-dos-early-code-open-sourced/) reports that Microsoft acquired 86-DOS for "just under $100,000" and shipped it to IBM as PC-DOS 1.0 in August 1981.

This release follows the 2024 publication of MS-DOS 4.0 source code, [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/microsoft_pc_dos_1_point_oh/) notes, but pushes the public archive further back than any prior Microsoft retro-code release.

## What We Don't Know

The Microsoft post and accompanying coverage do not address whether further early DOS materials remain unrecovered, nor whether additional snapshots of PC-DOS or 86-DOS will be added to the same repository in the future. Reactions from the broader community were still arriving in the days after the release; [OSnews](https://www.osnews.com/story/144849/earliest-86-dos-and-pc-dos-code-released-as-open-source/) editor Thom Holwerda described the situation succinctly: "It's wild that the source code had to be transcribed from paper, including notes and changes."
